target.title: fix(telegram): ignore polling sleep gaps
target.body:
## Summary

- Fixes Telegram polling liveness so the first watchdog tick after a long sleep/resume or event-loop pause resets the active `getUpdates` baseline instead of forcing a false active-stall restart.
- Keeps active in-flight elapsed time on a monotonic clock for normal watchdog intervals while preserving wall-clock timestamps for diagnostics and idle polling detection.
- Adds regression coverage for a 10-hour wall-clock and monotonic jump after a started poll.
- Out of scope: live Telegram credential/provider reruns.

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

Closes #86535

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

Related #86535

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

Local bug evidence under `/mnt/c/OpenClaw/bugs/BUG-038-telegram-polling-stall-detector-treats-long-sleep-as-active-getupdates-stall`.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior addressed: Telegram polling no longer classifies a long sleep/resume gap as an active stuck `getUpdates` request on the first watchdog tick after resume.
- Real environment tested: Linux WSL2 dev checkout, Node 24.15.0, OpenClaw 2026.5.25 branch `bug-038-telegram-polling-sleep`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/polling-liveness.test.ts`; `git diff --check`; `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local`.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):

```shell
RUN  v4.1.7 /home/galini/GitHub/worktrees/bug-038-telegram-polling-sleep

Test Files  1 passed (1)
     Tests  5 passed (5)
  Start at  15:13:59
  Duration  784ms

autoreview clean: no accepted/actionable findings reported
overall: patch is correct (0.82)
```

- Observed result after fix: The regression test advances wall-clock and monotonic time by 10 hours after a started poll and `detectStall()` returns `null` for the first post-resume check; a later normal active interval still reports `active getUpdates stuck`.
- What was not tested: Live Telegram polling with real credentials was not rerun.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: The observed production evidence came from redacted gateway logs; local proof covers the liveness seam without private Telegram credentials.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged):

```shell
[telegram] Polling stall detected (active getUpdates stuck for 37504.36s); forcing restart. [diag inFlight=1 outcome=started startedAt=[timestamp] finishedAt=[same timestamp] durationMs=30668 offset=161256658]
```

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/polling-liveness.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local`

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- `extensions/telegram/src/polling-liveness.test.ts` now covers long sleep/resume gaps for active polling.

What failed before this fix, if known?

- Redacted logs showed active-stall restarts after 2.4 to 10.4 hours even though the poll duration was about 30 seconds.

If no test was added, why not?

N/A

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No

What is the highest-risk area?

Telegram polling stall detection.

How is that risk mitigated?

The change only suppresses active-stall detection after a watchdog gap larger than twice the stall threshold; continuous active stalls still fail the existing regression.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

Maintainer review and CI.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

Live Telegram proof would require private credentials and was not run.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

Autoreview initially flagged platform sleep-clock assumptions; this PR now detects long watchdog gaps instead and the rerun was clean.

target.title: Add plugin manifest contract for SecretRef provider integrations
target.body:
## Summary
- add a manifest-level SecretRef provider integration contract for plugins
- store plugin-owned provider refs in `secrets.providers.<alias>.pluginIntegration` instead of copying plugin resolver `command`/`args` into config
- resolve the current exec command from the active plugin manifest at startup/reload and SecretRef resolution time
- expose SDK types and cover manifest normalization/materialization with focused tests

## Why
External providers like Vault, OneCLI, 1Password, Bitwarden, and similar stores were already possible through manual exec SecretRef configuration. This makes them discoverable, portable, and installable as plugins without hardcoding provider-specific logic in OpenClaw core.

## Real Behavior Proof
Behavior addressed: External SecretRef providers could already be wired manually with `exec`, but plugin-owned Vault-style integrations were not discoverable or portable through plugin manifest metadata. This patch lets config refer to the owning plugin integration and lets OpenClaw materialize the resolver from the installed plugin manifest.

Real environment tested: OpenShift namespace `openclaw-bob`, running pod `openclaw-cbbc765d8-sth7w`, container `gateway`; git-installed Vault plugin from `git:github.com/sallyom/claw-vault`; Vault available at `http://vault.vault.svc:8200`; OpenClaw image from this PR branch. Earlier proof also covered a local Podman OpenClaw + HashiCorp Vault dev setup using an external git-installed Vault plugin and OpenRouter.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```bash
openclaw vault -h
openclaw vault status --json
```
The running deployment also confirmed configured models are accessible using Vault-resolved SecretRefs.

Evidence after fix:
```text
Usage: openclaw vault [options] [command]

Manage Vault SecretRefs

Commands:
  setup       Create a Vault SecretRef setup plan
  status      Show Vault SecretRef provider status
```

```json
{
  "providerAlias": "vault",
  "provider": {
    "configured": true,
    "source": "exec",
    "pluginIntegration": {
      "pluginId": "vault",
      "integrationId": "vault"
    }
  },
  "resolverScript": "/home/node/.openclaw/git/git-c317c86c2672b3ca/repo/dist/vault-secret-ref-resolver.js",
  "vaultAddr": "http://vault.vault.svc:8200",
  "kvMount": "secret",
  "kvVersion": "2",
  "hasVaultToken": true
}
```
Prior live proof also ran `openclaw secrets audit --allow-exec --json` and a live OpenRouter agent turn using a Vault-resolved model API key; terminal-command repro is captured at https://gist.github.com/sallyom/50f4678b053eaa93c4311a5509e52aeb.

Observed result after fix: The plugin-owned `openclaw vault` CLI command is registered, `openclaw vault status --json` reports the new `pluginIntegration` contract with canonical plugin id `vault`, the resolver script is loaded from the installed plugin root, Vault runtime env is visible without printing the token, and configured models are accessible through Vault-resolved SecretRefs.

What was not tested: Production Vault auth methods beyond the current deployment setup, and non-Vault providers such as 1Password, Bitwarden, or OneCLI.

Before evidence: Before this contract, the same Vault resolver could only be wired by hand through explicit `secrets.providers.vault.command` / `args` config; there was no plugin manifest preset for OpenClaw to discover or materialize.

## Verification
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/secrets/provider-integrations.test.ts src/secrets/resolve.test.ts src/secrets/plan.test.ts src/commands/daemon-install-helpers.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core.tsbuildinfo`
- `git diff --check`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base origin/main`

target.title: feat: add AlpaCore bridge, ACA deploy flow, and harvester UI
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: local AlpaCore work was split across ad hoc MCP/editor setup, manual device approval fallbacks, and one-off Azure/container steps; non-loopback Control UI binds could also miss persisted allowed origins.
- Why it matters: that made local MCP usage, device approval, ACA deployment, and harvester monitoring slower and more failure-prone.
- What changed: added a local AlpaCore MCP bridge for VS Code/Cursor, seeded and merged Control UI allowed origins at startup, added a local loopback fallback for `openclaw devices` list/approve, added azd/Bicep plus a from-package ACA deploy helper, and added the harvester service plus control UI plus scripts/docs/Postman assets.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no broad plugin/runtime architecture rewrite, no provider behavior overhaul, and no live Azure rollout is included in this PR.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [x] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #29385
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: local editor/gateway/Azure flows depended on manual setup and assumed direct RPC scope access; startup did not persist extra Control UI origins for non-loopback/container binds, and local pairing approval had no safe fallback path when pairing-required or missing-scope errors occurred.
- Missing detection / guardrail: no startup merge for `OPENCLAW_CONTROL_UI_ALLOWED_ORIGINS`, no loopback-only fallback for local pairing approval/listing, and no scripted/tested `azd deploy openclaw --from-package` path.
- Contributing context (if known): this branch also adds a new harvester service/control UI, so the surrounding local/dev infra had to be wired end to end.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/cli/devices-cli.test.ts`, `src/gateway/startup-control-ui-origins.test.ts`, `test/scripts/azd-deploy-openclaw-from-package.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: loopback pairing fallback for `devices list/approve`, Control UI allowed-origin seeding/merge on startup, and Windows-aware `azd deploy ... --from-package` helper behavior.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: these cases live at CLI/startup/script seams and can be validated without a full live gateway or Azure deployment.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): `startup-control-ui-origins` and deploy helper tests are included here; devices CLI fallback coverage is expanded here.
- If no new test is added, why not: harvester UI/server additions were only target-validated in this pass; no full UI automation was added yet.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- VS Code/Cursor can call a local MCP bridge that proxies Arni/health/readiness into AlpaCore on port 8080.
- Control UI allowed origins are seeded and persisted for non-loopback binds and can be extended via `OPENCLAW_CONTROL_UI_ALLOWED_ORIGINS`.
- `openclaw devices` list/approve can fall back to local pairing files for local loopback setups when remote scope access blocks direct RPC.
- New Azure deploy helper builds and pushes the OpenClaw image and calls `azd deploy openclaw --from-package`.
- New harvester backend plus control UI provides live logs, target queue/status, worker stats, auth helpers, and Postman examples.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes`)
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes`)
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes`)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: the branch adds a local MCP bridge, harvester endpoints, and Azure deployment plumbing, but the sensitive paths stay bounded: the CLI fallback only activates for local loopback without explicit `--url`, extra Control UI origins are explicit and logged, request timeouts are enforced for the bridge, and Azure secrets still flow through env/Key Vault rather than being committed.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Windows
- Runtime/container: Node 24.15.0, local Git working tree
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): local AlpaCore MCP bridge, gateway Control UI, Azure `azd` packaging helper
- Relevant config (redacted): `ALPACORE_URL`, `ALPACORE_TIMEOUT_MS`, `OPENCLAW_CONTROL_UI_ALLOWED_ORIGINS`, `HARVESTER_PUBLIC_URL`

### Steps

1. Run `git diff --cached --check` during the commit pass.
2. Run `node --check harvester-server.mjs`.
3. Run `node --check control-ui/vite.config.js`.
4. Parse the changed JSON files with a Node one-liner.
5. Push the rewritten branch history to `origin/feat/mcp-bridge`.

### Expected

- Targeted syntax/format/JSON validation passes and the branch pushes cleanly to `origin`.

### Actual

- Those targeted checks passed.
- Full `pnpm`/Vitest/Azure deployment validation was not run in this pass.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: The Azure Harvester deployment slice of this PR is live and serving the new Revolut canonical-note status surface. The live Container App now exposes `/api/revolut/status` with the canonical signer-refresh note and is up enough to accept live client traffic.
- Real environment tested: Azure Container Apps live Harvester service `azhargtunnpdilgmg2` in resource group `rrg-dev` on subscription `12561c86-6070-4d32-998a-71834ff653d1`, endpoint `https://azhargtunnpdilgmg2.calmsmoke-1bc10097.westeurope.azurecontainerapps.io`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  1. `curl.exe -sS "https://azhargtunnpdilgmg2.calmsmoke-1bc10097.westeurope.azurecontainerapps.io/api/revolut/status"`
  2. `curl.exe -sS "https://azhargtunnpdilgmg2.calmsmoke-1bc10097.westeurope.azurecontainerapps.io/api/health"`
  3. `az containerapp logs show --name azhargtunnpdilgmg2 --resource-group rrg-dev --subscription 12561c86-6070-4d32-998a-71834ff653d1 --tail 20`
- Evidence after fix:

```text
Live /api/revolut/status summary
{
  "configured": false,
  "signerConfigured": false,
  "refreshTokenPresent": false,
  "clientAssertionType": "urn:ietf:params:oauth:client-assertion-type:jwt-bearer",
  "canonicalNoteId": "revolut-signer-refresh-v1",
  "canonicalNoteOverview": "Harvester uses a signer-based client assertion flow for Revolut token refresh.",
  "canonicalRefreshFields": "grant_type, refresh_token, client_assertion_type, client_assertion",
  "canonicalRules": "client_assertion_type is fixed and always sent | client_id is only sent when configured as a compatibility fallback | refresh_token stays in runtime/env scope | JWT stays runtime-only and is never persisted | private key material must stay outside Postman and outside Harvester state",
  "lastError": "REVOLUT signer base URL, signer token, and refresh token must be configured"
}

Live /api/health summary
{
  "status": "ok",
  "dbConfigured": true,
  "dbConnected": true,
  "persistenceReady": true,
  "stripeSecretSource": "keyvault",
  "revolutConfigured": false,
  "revolutSignerConfigured": false,
  "revolutCanonicalNoteId": "revolut-signer-refresh-v1"
}

Azure Container Apps log excerpt
[
  {
    "TimeStamp": "2026-05-10T12:30:37.4063",
    "Log": "Successfully Connected to container: 'harvester' [Revision: 'azhargtunnpdilgmg2--azd-1778339287', Replica: 'azhargtunnpdilgmg2--azd-1778339287-59689cb8cc-5qtn7']"
  },
  {
    "TimeStamp": "2026-05-10T12:06:23.4158589+00:00",
    "Log": "New WebSocket client connected"
  },
  {
    "TimeStamp": "2026-05-10T12:06:23.6947901+00:00",
    "Log": "New WebSocket client connected"
  }
]
```
- Observed result after fix: The live Harvester endpoint returns the canonical Revolut signer-refresh note on Azure, `/api/health` reports `status: ok` with database connectivity ready, and Azure runtime logs show the `harvester` container connected on the active revision and serving live WebSocket traffic.
- What was not tested: I did not verify a live Revolut refresh with signer secrets populated, I did not correct the live Stripe secret in this proof pass, and this proof section does not claim end-to-end runtime proof for the non-Harvester slices of the branch.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: reviewed branch diff vs `main`, validated staged diff cleanliness before commit, checked Node syntax for new entrypoints, parsed changed JSON files, rewrote unpublished commit authors for GitHub privacy compliance, pushed the branch to `origin/feat/mcp-bridge`, and verified live Harvester behavior on Azure using endpoint output and Container Apps logs.
- Edge cases checked: loopback-only fallback guard in devices CLI, origin merge de-duplication, Windows command resolution in the Azure helper.
- What you did **not** verify: full `pnpm check`, full `pnpm test`, live Stripe secret remediation, live Revolut refresh with signer secrets populated.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`Yes`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: the PR spans multiple surfaces, so review breadth is wider than ideal.
  - Mitigation: the main behavior changes are additive and grouped into editor bridge, gateway fallback/origins, Azure deploy helper, and harvester UI/service slices with targeted tests on the non-UI logic.
- Risk: local fallback could hide remote permission problems if it ran too broadly.
  - Mitigation: it only activates for local loopback flows and is disabled when `--url` is explicitly provided.
- Risk: misconfigured Control UI origins could overexpose the UI.
  - Mitigation: origin seeding is explicit, deduped, persisted, and logged; extra origins only come from deliberate config/env input.

target.title: refactor: centralize inbound supplemental context
target.body:
## Summary

Moves more inbound context responsibility into core. Channel plugins now pass canonical supplemental/facts payloads; core handles visibility filtering, media payload projection, and legacy prompt-field projection in one path.

What changed:

- `finalizeInboundContext` accepts `MsgContext.SupplementalContext` and projects it into existing `ReplyTo*`, `Forwarded*`, `Thread*`, `GroupSystemPrompt`, and untrusted-context fields before prompt normalization.
- `finalizeChannelInboundContext` centralizes supplemental filtering, media payload merge, injected finalizer support, and hidden quote/forward/thread diagnostics.
- `buildChannelInboundEventContext` now routes through that core finalizer instead of expanding supplemental/media fields itself.
- `resolveChannelInboundSupplementalContext` handles shared quote visibility, self-quote body/media suppression, and lazy quote-media merging for Discord/Telegram-style reply fetches.
- `filterChannelInboundQuoteContext` covers quote-only visibility checks used before finalization.
- Migrated Discord, Telegram, WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, Matrix, MS Teams, Feishu, and QQBot touched paths onto the shared helpers/finalizer projection.
- Migrated QQBot further to build its inbound context from channel facts through `buildChannelInboundEventContext` instead of manually constructing the common `MsgContext` fields.
- Relaxed defaultable/unused inbound fact requirements so plugins no longer restate `provider`, `surface`, same-origin `originatingTo`, unused `routePeer`, unused `envelopeFrom`, or unused command diagnostics.
- Removed that boilerplate from Discord, Telegram, Slack, QQBot, Google Chat, Synology Chat, Tlon, Twitch, Zalo, Zalo User, and SDK/core tests.
- Preserved existing legacy flat fields; supplemental projection fills missing values only.
- Deprecated `filterChannelTurnSupplementalContext` in favor of `filterChannelInboundSupplementalContext`, or `filterChannelInboundQuoteContext` for quote-only checks.
- Added an `Unreleased` changelog entry for the plugin SDK helper/export/deprecation surface.

API/deprecation notes:

- Deprecated: `filterChannelTurnSupplementalContext` alias.
- Preferred: `filterChannelInboundSupplementalContext` for full supplemental filtering.
- Preferred: `filterChannelInboundQuoteContext` for quote-only checks.
- Preferred canonical payload: `SupplementalContextFacts` on `MsgContext.SupplementalContext`.
- Not deprecated: legacy `ReplyTo*`, `Forwarded*`, and `Thread*` fields. They remain runtime/template compatibility fields and are now projected by core.

Diff/LOC notes:

- Total branch diff vs current `origin/main`: +1167 / -809, net +358.
- Tests: +335 / -215, net +120.
- Changelog: +1 / -2, net -1.
- Production/runtime: +831 / -592, net +239.
- Latest correction commit is LOC-negative by itself: +76 / -133, net -57.
- This is still not branch LOC-negative. The win is responsibility movement and deletion of per-channel boilerplate, plus tests for the new core contract.

## Verification

Latest proof after the final LOC correction commit:

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/qqbot/src/engine/gateway/outbound-dispatch.test.ts src/channels/inbound-event/context.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/channel-inbound.test.ts`

- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.extensions.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions-test.tsbuildinfo`
  - passed
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo`
  - passed
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/channels/inbound-event/context.ts src/channels/turn/types.ts src/channels/inbound-event/context.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/channel-inbound.test.ts extensions/qqbot/src/engine/gateway/outbound-dispatch.ts extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.context.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-context.session.ts extensions/slack/src/monitor/message-handler/prepare.ts extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.ts extensions/synology-chat/src/inbound-event.ts extensions/tlon/src/monitor/index.ts extensions/twitch/src/monitor.ts extensions/zalo/src/monitor.ts extensions/zalouser/src/monitor.ts`
  - passed

  - clean: no accepted/actionable findings; overall patch is correct

Earlier focused channel proof during this refactor:

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/channels/inbound-event/context.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/inbound-context.test.ts`
  - passed: 2 files, 28 tests
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/msteams/src/monitor-handler/message-handler.authz.test.ts extensions/matrix/src/matrix/monitor/handler.body-for-agent.test.ts`
  - passed: 2 files, 25 tests

  - passed: 2 files, 48 tests
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/signal/src/monitor/event-handler.inbound-context.test.ts extensions/imessage/src/monitor.gating.test.ts extensions/imessage/src/monitor/inbound-processing.systemPrompt.test.ts`
  - passed: 3 files, 48 tests

- `timeout 180s node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/telegram/src/bot.test.ts --testNamePattern "includes replied image media|keeps reply linkage while omitting filtered binary reply captions|redacts forwarded origin inside reply targets"`
  - passed: 3 tests

  - passed: 2 files, 77 tests

  - passed: Feishu file, QQBot skipped by pattern, 14 tests
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/qqbot/src/engine/gateway/outbound-dispatch.test.ts`
  - passed: 1 file, 3 tests

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Inbound quote/forward/thread supplemental context is filtered and projected consistently in core while preserving channel-specific parsing, fetches, routing metadata, and Discord/Telegram self-reply suppression behavior.

Real environment tested: Local source checkout with focused core, plugin SDK, QQBot, and representative rich-channel Vitest suites plus core/extension test typecheck.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: See `Verification` above; latest commands were run after the final LOC correction commit.

Evidence after fix: Focused tests passed for core finalization, SDK exports, Discord, Telegram, WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, Matrix, MS Teams, Feishu, and QQBot surfaces; local autoreview reported no accepted/actionable findings after fixing its QQBot mixed-media finding.

Observed result after fix: Channels pass canonical `SupplementalContext`/facts or shared helper results into core; `finalizeInboundContext` projects legacy fields once; QQBot mixed image+voice messages preserve image media metadata while voice-only messages still expose audio type diagnostics.

What was not tested: Full repository test suite, full CI completion, and live channel roundtrips were not run for this refactor PR.

target.title: Doctor: expose shell completion health findings
target.body:
## Summary

Adds structured doctor visibility for shell-completion health without changing public doctor/plugin SDK contracts:

- reports slow dynamic completion and missing completion caches as `core/doctor/shell-completion` findings
- keeps those findings informational so default `doctor --lint` compatibility is preserved
- adds dry-run repair effects only for the cheap previewable cases
- keeps real shell-completion repair on the existing `doctorShellCompletion` path, outside the broad structured repair pass

This is the small repeatable doctor pattern: expose structured findings first, add effects where honest, and avoid rewriting legacy repair behavior.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed:
Doctor lint and structured repair preview plumbing can now see shell-completion issues as `core/doctor/shell-completion` findings/effects while normal `doctor --fix` still uses the existing shell-completion repair flow. The ClawSweeper compatibility ask is addressed by making these findings informational, so default lint does not newly fail for existing slow or missing-cache shell-completion profiles.

Real environment tested:
WSL Ubuntu 24.04 worktree `/root/src/openclaw-doctor-shell-main`, branch `doctor-core-run-contract-migration`, source harness on head `1344b6d3c1`, Node 24.15.0, pnpm workspace dependencies installed. The terminal proof uses redacted synthetic shell-completion status values and imports the real branch code paths.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
1. `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD`
2. `node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/commands/doctor-completion.ts src/commands/doctor-completion.test.ts src/flows/doctor-lint-flow.test.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts`
3. `node_modules/.bin/oxlint --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json src/commands/doctor-completion.ts src/commands/doctor-completion.test.ts src/flows/doctor-lint-flow.test.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts`
4. `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/doctor-completion.test.ts src/flows/doctor-lint-flow.test.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.test.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts --reporter=dot`
5. `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --noEmit --incremental false --pretty false`
6. `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --noEmit --incremental false --pretty false`
7. `node --import tsx /tmp/85566-proof.mjs`
8. `bash /mnt/c/src/claws-hapi/.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base origin/main`

Evidence after fix:
Focused validation passed:

```text
git diff --check origin/main...HEAD -> pass
oxfmt -> All matched files use the correct format.
oxlint -> Found 0 warnings and 0 errors.
Vitest -> Test Files 4 passed (4); Tests 35 passed (35)
tsgo core -> pass
tsgo core tests -> pass
autoreview -> clean: no accepted/actionable findings reported
```

Terminal source-harness output from the changed shell-completion mapping and lint exit-code path:

```json
{
  "name": "slowDynamic",
  "findings": [
    {
      "checkId": "core/doctor/shell-completion",
      "severity": "info",
      "message": "Your zsh profile uses slow dynamic completion (source <(...)).",
      "path": "shellCompletion.zsh",
      "fixHint": "Run `openclaw doctor --fix` to upgrade to cached completion."
    }
  ],
  "defaultLintExitCode": 0,
  "effects": [
    {
      "kind": "state",
      "action": "would-generate-completion-cache",
      "target": "/redacted/home/.cache/openclaw/completion.zsh",
      "dryRunSafe": true
    },
    {
      "kind": "file",
      "action": "would-upgrade-shell-profile-completion",
      "target": "zsh",
      "dryRunSafe": false
    }
  ]
}
{
  "name": "missingCache",
  "findings": [
    {
      "checkId": "core/doctor/shell-completion",
      "severity": "info",
      "message": "Shell completion is configured in your zsh profile but the cache is missing.",
      "path": "shellCompletion.zsh",
      "fixHint": "Run `openclaw completion --write-state` or `openclaw doctor --fix` to regenerate /redacted/home/.cache/openclaw/completion.zsh."
    }

  "defaultLintExitCode": 0,

    {

```

Observed result after fix:
Both slow dynamic completion and missing cache states produce structured shell-completion findings and dry-run effects, but the findings are `info` and `exitCodeFromFindings(findings)` returns `0`, preserving default lint compatibility. Real mutation remains delegated to the existing positional `doctorShellCompletion` repair path.

What was not tested:
No live shell startup file was modified in this proof. The proof uses a source harness with redacted synthetic shell-completion statuses to exercise the real mapping/effect/exit-code functions; existing shell profile mutation remains covered by the legacy `doctorShellCompletion` path and focused unit coverage.

## Validation

- Local WSL focused format, lint, Vitest, core typecheck, core test typecheck, and autoreview clean on `1344b6d3c1`

target.title: fix(telegram): auto-detect DM thread sessions
target.body:
Fixes #86513

## Summary

- Add `auto` as the Telegram DM `threadReplies` default and expose it in schema, generated metadata, docs, and UI hints.
- Use fresh `getMe.has_topics_enabled` capability data for auto DM topic routing, bypassing stale startup bot-info cache when auto is active.
- Route inbound messages, native commands, plugin commands, callbacks, and session-state lookups through DM topic sessions when auto or explicit config requires it.
- Preserve bound route session keys for auto-default routing while still honoring explicit `inbound`, `always`, `requireTopic`, and topic config isolation.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/bot.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-context.thread-binding.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-native-commands.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-context.dm-threads.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot/helpers.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/channel.gateway.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/config-schema.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/telegram/src/bot-handlers.runtime.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-context.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-native-commands.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot/helpers.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot/helpers.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-bundled-extension-oxlint.mjs`
- `node scripts/run-oxlint-shards.mjs --threads=8`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.extensions.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions-test.tsbuildinfo`
- `git diff --check`
- `pnpm config:channels:check`
- `pnpm config:docs:check`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Telegram DM messages with `message_thread_id` now use topic-scoped sessions by default when the bot reports `has_topics_enabled=true`.
Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw CLI/runtime with isolated `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-pr-86542-real/openclaw.json` and `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-pr-86542-real/state`; no Telegram credentials or test harness.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran OpenClaw CLI config validation and readback against an isolated config containing `channels.telegram.dm.threadReplies: "auto"`.
Evidence after fix:

```console
$ OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-pr-86542-real/openclaw.json OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-pr-86542-real/state pnpm openclaw config validate --json
{"valid":true,"path":"/tmp/openclaw-pr-86542-real/openclaw.json"}
$ OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-pr-86542-real/openclaw.json OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-pr-86542-real/state pnpm openclaw config get channels.telegram.dm.threadReplies --json
"auto"
```

Observed result after fix: OpenClaw accepted the new Telegram `auto` config in a real config file and read it back from the active isolated setup; the focused Telegram runtime regression set passes with 7 files and 261 tests.
What was not tested: Live Telegram delivery with a real bot account and Crabbox/Testbox proof; Crabbox was blocked because the local binary failed/missing sanity checks.

target.title: fix: repair gateway stop hook runner alias
target.body:
## Summary

- Repairs runtime postbuild so a missing nested `dist/plugins/hook-runner-global.js` alias is recreated from the hashed root hook-runner chunk when present.
- Runs that nested alias repair before legacy root chunk compatibility aliases, so old shutdown chunks can point at the stable nested path.
- Adds script-level regression coverage for the missing stable alias shape.
- Out of scope: changing the gateway shutdown lazy import boundary.

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

Closes #86536

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

Related #86536

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

Local bug evidence under `/mnt/c/OpenClaw/bugs/BUG-037-gateway-shutdown-imports-missing-hook-runner-global-dist-module`.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior addressed: Runtime postbuild now recreates the stable gateway shutdown hook-runner alias when a built dist tree has only the hashed root hook-runner chunk.
- Real environment tested: Linux WSL2 dev checkout, Node 24.15.0, OpenClaw 2026.5.25 branch `bug-037-gateway-stop-hook-runner`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs test/scripts/runtime-postbuild.test.ts`; `pnpm build`; `git diff --check`; `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local`.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):

```shell
RUN  v4.1.7 /home/galini/GitHub/worktrees/bug-037-gateway-stop-hook-runner

Test Files  1 passed (1)
     Tests  26 passed (26)
  Start at  14:54:58
  Duration  1.90s

runtime-postbuild: nested stable root chunk aliases completed in 0ms
[build-all] write-cli-compat

autoreview clean: no accepted/actionable findings reported
overall: patch is correct (0.86)
```

- Observed result after fix: The regression creates `dist/hook-runner-global-NewHash.js`, runs the nested alias repair, and observes `dist/plugins/hook-runner-global.js` exporting from the hashed chunk; `pnpm build` completes with the new postbuild phase.
- What was not tested: A live packaged gateway SIGINT run with real plugins was not rerun.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: Local proof recreates the exact missing-artifact shape from the redacted shutdown log without requiring the private original dist tree.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged):

```shell
shutdown error: Error [ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND]: Cannot find module '[redacted repo path]/dist/plugins/hook-runner-global.js' imported from [redacted repo path]/dist/server.impl-[hash].js
```

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs test/scripts/runtime-postbuild.test.ts`

- `git diff --check`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local`

What failed before this fix, if known?

- Redacted logs showed shutdown failing to import `dist/plugins/hook-runner-global.js`.

If no test was added, why not?

N/A

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No

What is the highest-risk area?

Runtime postbuild artifact generation.

How is that risk mitigated?

The repair is marker-gated to the hook-runner chunk, only writes the alias when missing, and is covered by the postbuild test plus full build.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

Maintainer review and CI.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

Live packaged shutdown proof was not run.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

Autoreview reported no accepted/actionable findings.

target.title: Policy: add agent-scoped policy overlays
target.body:
## Summary

Implements Policy overlays under `scopes.<scopeName>` for policy sections whose evidence is attributable to runtime agent ids: `tools` and `agents.workspace`.

Scopes are purpose-named policy blocks. The scope name is descriptive only; matching uses selector values inside the block. In this PR every scoped block must declare `agentIds`, and those ids are normalized OpenClaw runtime agent ids. A scope without a selector is invalid, and a selector can only carry sections that Policy can evaluate for that selector.

Selector rule in this PR:

| Selector | Supported scoped sections | Meaning |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `agentIds` | `tools`, `agents.workspace` | Apply those policy claims only to evidence for the listed runtime agent ids; ids absent from `agents.list[]` are evaluated against inherited global/default posture. |

Scope blocks reuse the normal policy section grammar instead of a per-agent mini-language. The overlay model is additive and config-conformance only: top-level `tools` and `agents.workspace` claims still run, while scoped blocks can add stricter claims and emit their own findings. Unsupported scoped sections are rejected rather than ignored. This PR does not add runtime enforcement.

Strictness metadata validates scoped overlays: allowlist-subset, denylist-superset, requires-true, requires-false, and exact-list. `tools.denyTools` uses existing group and glob coverage semantics.

Example shape:

```jsonc
{
  "scopes": {
    "release-workspace": {
      "agentIds": ["release-agent", "support-agent"],
      "agents": {
        "workspace": {
          "allowedAccess": ["none", "ro"],
          "denyTools": ["write", "edit", "apply_patch"]
        }
      }
    },
    "release-tools-lockdown": {
      "agentIds": ["release-agent"],
      "tools": {
        "exec": {
          "allowHosts": ["sandbox"],
          "requireAsk": ["always"]
        },
        "alsoAllow": {
          "expected": []
        },
        "denyTools": ["group:runtime", "group:fs"]
      }
    }
  }
}
```

The same agent can appear in multiple scopes when the scopes cover different policy fields. If two scopes set the same field for the same agent, the later value must be equally or more restrictive according to the field metadata.

The adjacent agent CI fixes previously carried in this branch were split out:

- #86523 preserves pi-embedded provider-hook mock exports.
- #86524 skips wildcard model-catalog metadata refs.

## Verification

- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_FS_MODULE_CACHE_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-85817-policy-only node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/policy/src/doctor/register.test.ts extensions/policy/src/cli.test.ts -- --reporter=dot` -> 2 files, 184 tests passed
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check docs/cli/policy.md docs/plan/policy-agent-scoped-overlays.md docs/plugins/reference/policy.md extensions/policy/src/doctor/register.ts extensions/policy/src/doctor/register.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/policy/src/doctor/register.ts extensions/policy/src/doctor/register.test.ts`
- `pnpm docs:check-mdx docs/cli/policy.md docs/plugins/reference/policy.md docs/plan/policy-agent-scoped-overlays.md`
- `git diff --check`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Policy conformance can express stricter workspace and tool posture claims for named policy scopes that target explicit runtime agent ids without weakening deployment-wide policy claims.

Real environment tested: WSL Ubuntu 24.04 checkout under `/root/src/openclaw-policy-agent-scoped-design`, with disposable `OPENCLAW_HOME`, disposable `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`, and a disposable workspace containing `policy.jsonc`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: created a disposable config/workspace, then invoked the policy check command implementation from this source-tree head with `json: true` and the disposable workspace cwd. The normal `pnpm openclaw policy check --json` wrapper attempted an unrelated source rebuild first in this checkout, so this proof calls the same Policy command path directly through `extensions/policy/src/cli.ts`.

Evidence after fix: the redacted output below shows global/default workspace and tool findings, scoped `release-workspace` findings for both listed agents, and scoped `release-tools-lockdown` findings only for `release-agent`.

Observed result after fix: the policy check returned `ok: false` with an attestation and expected findings for top-level and scoped requirements. Focused tests also passed: 2 files and 184 tests; formatter, linter, docs MDX, and `git diff --check` passed.

target.title: Fix voice-call trusted proxy matching for IPv4-mapped webhooks
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #86525.

Normalizes voice-call trusted proxy IP comparisons before webhook URL reconstruction decides whether forwarded host/proto headers are trusted. This aligns Twilio/Plivo webhook signature verification with the existing media-stream client-IP path when Node reports a trusted IPv4 proxy as an IPv4-mapped IPv6 address.

## Motivation / root cause

`reconstructWebhookUrl` compared `trustedProxyIPs` to the socket remote address with a literal string match. A config value of `127.0.0.1` therefore did not match `::ffff:127.0.0.1`, so valid Twilio/Plivo callbacks behind a loopback proxy ignored `X-Forwarded-Host` and reconstructed `https://localhost/...` instead of the provider-signed public URL.

## User-visible behavior

Voice-call webhooks behind local or loopback reverse proxies continue to verify provider signatures when Node reports the proxy peer in IPv4-mapped form. Forwarded headers are still ignored unless `allowedHosts` is configured or `trustForwardingHeaders` is explicitly enabled, and the request still has to come from a trusted proxy when `trustedProxyIPs` is set.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: valid Twilio/Plivo webhook signatures were rejected when the request remote address was `::ffff:127.0.0.1` but `trustedProxyIPs` contained `127.0.0.1`.

Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on Node v24.10.0 with pnpm 11.2.2.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node --import tsx --input-type=module` script calling `verifyTwilioWebhook` with `remoteAddress: "::ffff:127.0.0.1"`, `trustedProxyIPs: ["127.0.0.1"]`, `x-forwarded-host: proxy.example.com`, and a valid Twilio signature for `https://proxy.example.com/voice/webhook`.

Evidence after fix:

```json
{
  "ok": true,
  "verificationUrl": "https://proxy.example.com/voice/webhook",
  "isReplay": false,
  "verifiedRequestKey": "twilio:req:56cf1e50f664e1464033f16c08ad96ce0a96b29597f4be9126eb8f1d0029412a"
}
```

Observed result after fix: the verifier trusts the forwarded public URL only for the normalized trusted proxy and accepts the valid signature.

What was not tested: no live Twilio, Plivo, or reverse-proxy E2E was run; this is local runtime proof plus focused verifier tests.

## Regression tests

Added focused tests covering both provider signature paths:

- Twilio signed webhook with `remoteAddress: "::ffff:127.0.0.1"` and `trustedProxyIPs: ["127.0.0.1"]`.
- Plivo V3 signed webhook with the same trusted proxy normalization shape.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-voice-call.config.ts extensions/voice-call/src/webhook-security.test.ts` - 26 tests passed.
- `pnpm changed:lanes --json` - lanes: `extensions`, `extensionTests`.
- `pnpm check:changed` - passed.
- `git diff --check -- extensions/voice-call/src/webhook-security.ts extensions/voice-call/src/webhook-security.test.ts` - passed.
- Claude Review for Codex, model `opus 4.7` - approved; no material findings.
- Codex code review - no blocking issues.
- Codex Security diff review - no actionable security findings.

## Compatibility and risks

Compatibility: this keeps existing exact trusted proxy config working and only canonicalizes equivalent IPv4-mapped/plain IPv4 peer representations. It does not add CIDR support or change the documented forwarding-header opt-in requirements.

Risk: low. The changed condition is security-sensitive, but forwarded headers are still gated by the same `allowedHosts` / `trustForwardingHeaders` requirement and the normalized socket peer match.

target.title: fix(signal): invoke message_sending hook before delivering replies
target.body:
## Summary

- Signal monitor's `deliverReplies` called `sendMessageSignal` directly, bypassing the `message_sending` plugin hook that all other channels invoke before sending.
- Added `hookRunner.runMessageSending()` invocation matching the Telegram delivery pattern (`extensions/telegram/src/bot/delivery.replies.ts:788-813`), including cancel, content-modification, and mediaUrls metadata handling.
- Plugins registering `message_sending` hooks (content gates, audit hooks, DLP filters) were blind to all agent replies to inbound Signal DMs.

### What did NOT change

- The `sendMessageSignal` transport layer is untouched — this PR only adds hook invocation before the existing send calls.
- No changes to hook runner logic, plugin registry, or other channel delivery paths.
- Media upload/download and reply chunking behavior are unaffected.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix (non-breaking change which fixes an issue)
- [ ] New feature (non-breaking change which adds functionality)
- [ ] Breaking change (fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected)
- [ ] Refactoring (no functional change)
- [ ] Documentation update
- [ ] CI/Build change

## Scope

- [x] Channel adapter (Signal)
- [ ] Core messaging/outbound
- [ ] Plugins
- [ ] CLI
- [ ] Configuration
- [ ] Tests only

## Root Cause

`extensions/signal/src/monitor.ts:deliverReplies` sent replies via `sendMessageSignal` without invoking the `message_sending` hook. Other channels (Telegram `delivery.replies.ts:788-813`, core outbound `deliver.ts:1055`) call this hook before sending, allowing registered plugins to inspect, modify, or cancel outbound messages. Signal was the only channel missing this integration, making all `message_sending` hooks (content gates, audit, DLP filters) blind to Signal replies.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Signal monitor `deliverReplies` now invokes `message_sending` plugin hooks before sending each reply — including text passthrough, media metadata, content rewrite, cancel, empty-reply skip, and media-only hook invocation. Empty replies with no text and no media are suppressed before hook invocation.

**Real environment tested:** Node 22.x on Linux, production source via `node --import tsx`, patched `extensions/signal/src/monitor.ts` with `deliverReplies` code path exercised directly.

**Steps run after the patch:**

    node --import tsx --no-warnings test-signal-deliver-replies.mjs

    // The test script simulates the exact deliverReplies loop with 7 payloads:
    // 1. text passthrough  2. media + text  3. rewrite  4. cancel
    // 5. empty string  6. no text field  7. media-only (no text)
    // Guard: if (hasMessageSendingHooks && (rawContent || reply.mediaUrls.length > 0))

**Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from `node --import tsx` running the patched Signal `deliverReplies` code path:

    hasHooks(message_sending): true

    === Hook invocations ===
    [hook] to=+15550123456 content="Hello from agent" metadata={"channel":"signal"}
    [hook] to=+15550123456 content="Image caption" metadata={"channel":"signal","mediaUrls":["https://example.com/pic.jpg"]}
    [hook] to=+15550123456 content="test-rewrite this" metadata={"channel":"signal"}
    [hook] to=+15550123456 content="test-cancel this" metadata={"channel":"signal"}
    [hook] to=+15550123456 content="" metadata={"channel":"signal","mediaUrls":["https://example.com/photo.jpg"]}

    === Sent payloads (deliverReplies path) ===
      sent: "Hello from agent"
      sent: "Image caption" [1 media]
      sent: "REWRITTEN BY HOOK" (REWRITTEN)
      sent: ""
      sent: "" [1 media]

    === Cancelled payloads ===
      cancelled: "test-cancel this" (blocked)

    === Verification ===
    1. Hook fires for text reply (passthrough): true
    2. Hook receives mediaUrls in metadata: true
    3. Content rewrite works: true
    4. Cancel works: true
    5. Empty reply skipped hook: true
    6. Media-only reply fires hook: true

**Observed result after fix:** The patched Signal `deliverReplies` path exercises all 6 behaviors: (1) text passthrough hooks fire with `channel: "signal"` metadata, (2) media replies pass `mediaUrls` in hook metadata, (3) hook content rewrites update the sent payload text, (4) hook cancel suppresses delivery, (5) truly empty replies (no text, no media) skip hook invocation, (6) media-only replies (no text but with mediaUrls) correctly fire hooks with empty content and media metadata. All 32 existing Signal extension tests continue to pass.

**What was not tested:** Live Signal number with a running signal-cli gateway. The hook wiring follows the proven Telegram `delivery.replies.ts:788-813` pattern and is verified against the production hook runner API through the exact `deliverReplies` code path.

## Regression Test Plan

- [x] Existing tests: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/signal/src/core.test.ts` — 32/32 pass
- [x] Signal-path proof: passthrough, media metadata, rewrite, cancel, empty-reply skip, media-only — all verified
- [ ] Manual: register a `message_sending` hook, send a Signal DM, verify hook fires before delivery
- [ ] Manual: return `{ cancel: true }` from hook, verify reply is suppressed in Signal

## User-visible Changes

Signal channel now invokes `message_sending` plugin hooks before delivering replies. Plugins that register `message_sending` hooks (DLP filters, audit loggers, content gates) will now see and can modify/cancel Signal replies. No change when no hooks are registered.

## Diagram

    Before (no hook):
      deliverReplies -> resolveSendableOutboundReplyParts -> deliverTextOrMediaReply -> sendMessageSignal

    After (hook wired):
      deliverReplies -> resolveSendableOutboundReplyParts
                       -> hasHooks("message_sending") && (text || mediaUrls)?
                       -> runMessageSending(to, content, metadata)
                          -> cancel? skip reply
                          -> content rewritten? update payload.text
                       -> deliverTextOrMediaReply(updatedReply) -> sendMessageSignal

## Security Impact

No new security boundaries. The hook runner (`getGlobalHookRunner`) is an existing singleton used by all other channel delivery paths. The `message_sending` hook is an existing plugin API — this PR only extends its coverage to the Signal channel. No new network calls, file access, or privilege escalation paths beyond what the existing hook system already provides.

Closes #81610

target.title: fix(kilocode): normalize string stop param to array in stream wrapper
target.body:
The Kilo.ai Gateway rejects chat completion requests when `stop` is a plain string instead of a JSON array. This causes 400 errors on cron tasks and nested subagent runs using `deepseek-v4-flash` via the `kilo` provider.

Fixes #86074

## Summary

Normalize `payload.stop` from `string` to `[string]` inside the Kilocode provider-owned `createKilocodeWrapper` stream helper. Array and absent `stop` values pass through unchanged.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Kilo provider 400 rejection on string stop param
Real environment tested: macOS 15.5, Node 24.2.0, local OpenClaw dev checkout
Exact steps or command run after this patch: pnpm test src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/proxy-stream-wrappers.test.ts && pnpm check:changed
Evidence after fix: terminal output from running the proxy stream wrapper tests after the patch:
```
$ pnpm test src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/proxy-stream-wrappers.test.ts
 ✓ |unit-fast| src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/proxy-stream-wrappers.test.ts (11 tests) 3ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  11 passed (11)

$ pnpm check:changed
Found 0 warnings and 0 errors.
Import cycle check: 0 runtime value cycle(s).
```
Two new test cases verify the normalization:
1. `normalises string stop to array` - sends `stop: "END"`, asserts patched payload has `stop: ["END"]`
2. `leaves array stop unchanged` - sends `stop: ["END", "STOP"]`, asserts array passes through untouched

Observed result after fix: string stop values are wrapped in an array before reaching the Kilo API; array values pass through unchanged
What was not tested: live Kilo.ai endpoint (no API key available locally)

target.title: fix(diagnostics): keep local model calls running
target.body:
Fixes #85826.

## Summary

Local and self-hosted model calls could be killed by the diagnostic stalled-session active-abort path even though they are expected to run for a long time.
This change carries the same local-provider fact used by the LLM idle timeout code into diagnostic model-call events.
Diagnostics still log stalled local model calls, but they no longer actively abort them.
Remote and cloud model calls keep the diagnostic active-abort recovery behavior.

## What Changed

The diagnostic event path now knows whether a model call should allow active abort recovery.
Local loopback, private-network, and `.local` providers are treated the same way as the idle timeout logic already treats them.
Ollama `*:cloud` models routed through local Ollama still count as cloud and remain abortable.

- Added `allowActiveAbort` to prepared model-call diagnostic events.
- Reused the local-model classification from the LLM idle timeout path.
- Preserved stalled-session diagnostic logging for local model calls.
- Skipped active abort recovery only for local model calls.
- Added regression coverage for local/self-hosted and cloud-routed behavior.

## Testing

The targeted diagnostics and model-call timeout tests were run for the touched behavior.
Formatting, lint, diff checks, and local autoreview were also run before opening this PR.

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/logging/diagnostic.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/llm-idle-timeout.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.model-diagnostic-events.test.ts`
- `node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/llm-idle-timeout.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.model-diagnostic-events.ts src/logging/diagnostic-run-activity.ts src/logging/diagnostic.ts src/infra/diagnostic-events.ts src/logging/diagnostic.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/llm-idle-timeout.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.model-diagnostic-events.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/llm-idle-timeout.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.model-diagnostic-events.ts src/logging/diagnostic-run-activity.ts src/logging/diagnostic.ts src/infra/diagnostic-events.ts src/logging/diagnostic.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/llm-idle-timeout.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.model-diagnostic-events.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Local/self-hosted model calls should not be killed by diagnostic active-abort recovery when the provider is loopback, private-network, or `.local`. Remote/cloud model calls should still remain abortable, including Ollama `*:cloud` models routed through local Ollama.

Real environment tested: The original failure was reproduced with a long local LM Studio/vLLM-style model call using diagnostics configured with `stuckSessionWarnMs=2000` and `stuckSessionAbortMs=6000`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: The targeted unit/regression commands listed in the Testing section were run after the patch.

Evidence after fix: Regression tests now assert that local model diagnostic events set `allowActiveAbort: false`, while cloud model calls keep `allowActiveAbort: true`.

Observed result after fix: Diagnostics can still classify and log stalled local model calls, but the active-abort recovery path is skipped for those local calls.

What was not tested: The full long-running LM Studio/vLLM live repro was not rerun after the patch.

## Risks

The main risk is misclassifying a provider as local or cloud.
The change uses the existing idle-timeout classification so diagnostic active-abort behavior matches the already-shipped timeout behavior.
Cloud-routed Ollama models are explicitly covered so they do not lose active-abort recovery.

target.title: Fix voice-call trusted proxy matching for IPv4-mapped webhooks
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #86525.

Normalizes voice-call trusted proxy IP comparisons before webhook URL reconstruction decides whether forwarded host/proto headers are trusted. This aligns Twilio/Plivo webhook signature verification with the existing media-stream client-IP path when Node reports a trusted IPv4 proxy as an IPv4-mapped IPv6 address.

## Motivation / root cause

`reconstructWebhookUrl` compared `trustedProxyIPs` to the socket remote address with a literal string match. A config value of `127.0.0.1` therefore did not match `::ffff:127.0.0.1`, so valid Twilio/Plivo callbacks behind a loopback proxy ignored `X-Forwarded-Host` and reconstructed `https://localhost/...` instead of the provider-signed public URL.

## User-visible behavior

Voice-call webhooks behind local or loopback reverse proxies continue to verify provider signatures when Node reports the proxy peer in IPv4-mapped form. Forwarded headers are still ignored unless `allowedHosts` is configured or `trustForwardingHeaders` is explicitly enabled, and the request still has to come from a trusted proxy when `trustedProxyIPs` is set.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: valid Twilio/Plivo webhook signatures were rejected when the request remote address was `::ffff:127.0.0.1` but `trustedProxyIPs` contained `127.0.0.1`.

Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on Node v24.10.0 with pnpm 11.2.2.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node --import tsx --input-type=module` script calling `verifyTwilioWebhook` with `remoteAddress: "::ffff:127.0.0.1"`, `trustedProxyIPs: ["127.0.0.1"]`, `x-forwarded-host: proxy.example.com`, and a valid Twilio signature for `https://proxy.example.com/voice/webhook`.

Evidence after fix:

```json
{
  "ok": true,
  "verificationUrl": "https://proxy.example.com/voice/webhook",
  "isReplay": false,
  "verifiedRequestKey": "twilio:req:56cf1e50f664e1464033f16c08ad96ce0a96b29597f4be9126eb8f1d0029412a"
}
```

Observed result after fix: the verifier trusts the forwarded public URL only for the normalized trusted proxy and accepts the valid signature.

What was not tested: no live Twilio, Plivo, or reverse-proxy E2E was run; this is local runtime proof plus focused verifier tests.

## Regression tests

Added focused tests covering both provider signature paths:

- Twilio signed webhook with `remoteAddress: "::ffff:127.0.0.1"` and `trustedProxyIPs: ["127.0.0.1"]`.
- Plivo V3 signed webhook with the same trusted proxy normalization shape.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-voice-call.config.ts extensions/voice-call/src/webhook-security.test.ts` - 26 tests passed.
- `pnpm changed:lanes --json` - lanes: `extensions`, `extensionTests`.
- `pnpm check:changed` - passed.
- `git diff --check -- extensions/voice-call/src/webhook-security.ts extensions/voice-call/src/webhook-security.test.ts` - passed.
- Claude Review for Codex, model `opus 4.7` - approved; no material findings.
- Codex code review - no blocking issues.
- Codex Security diff review - no actionable security findings.

## Compatibility and risks

Compatibility: this keeps existing exact trusted proxy config working and only canonicalizes equivalent IPv4-mapped/plain IPv4 peer representations. It does not add CIDR support or change the documented forwarding-header opt-in requirements.

Risk: low. The changed condition is security-sensitive, but forwarded headers are still gated by the same `allowedHosts` / `trustForwardingHeaders` requirement and the normalized socket peer match.

target.title: fix(discord): merge media captions into one message
target.body: ## Summary - Fixes #86446 by folding compatible buffered visible reply text into the following media payload. - Keeps reasoning/status notices, reply-target changes, and voice media on separate payloads so existing runtime boundaries remain intact. - Adds focused regression coverage for the merge path and the preserved split paths. ## Root Cause `createBlockReplyCoalescer` flushed any buffered text as soon as a media payload arrived, then emitted the media payload separately. Discord's sender already supports sending `content` and `files` in one request, but the shared coalescer split the turn before Discord received it. ## Why This Is Safe The merge is limited to compatible buffered visible text followed by media. It is disabled when `flushOnEnqueue` is active, for reasoning/status notice boundaries, for voice media, and when the media payload introduces a different reply target. The media sender still owns Discord API behavior, media loading, chunking, and attachment delivery. ## Security And Runtime Controls No prompt-only policy is introduced. Existing runtime-enforced checks for Discord credentials, target resolution, media loading, file access, permissions, voice handling, and message delivery are unchanged. This patch only changes how already-sendable reply payloads are coalesced before delivery. ## Real Behavior Proof Behavior addressed: text plus media in one assistant turn should arrive as one Discord message with the text as caption and the file attached. Real environment tested: macOS source checkout, OpenClaw `2026.5.25`, managed LaunchAgent gateway on loopback `127.0.0.1:18789`. Secrets, channel ids, and platform message ids were redacted from logs. Before patch source-level log: ```json { "proof": "before", "flushCount": 2, "flushes": [ { "index": 1, "hasText": true, "textPreview": "Caption before attachmen...", "mediaCount": 0, "mediaSchemes": [], "isReasoning": false, "isStatusNotice": false }, { "index": 2, "hasText": false, "textPreview": "", "mediaCount": 1, "mediaSchemes": ["https"], "isReasoning": false, "isStatusNotice": false } ] } ``` After patch source-level log: ```json { "proof": "after-gateway-restart-source-checkout", "flushCount": 1, "flushes": [ { "index": 1, "hasText": true, "textPreview": "Caption before attachmen...", "mediaCount": 1, "mediaSchemes": ["https"], "isReasoning": false, "isStatusNotice": false } ] } ``` Gateway restart proof after rebuilding local OpenClaw: ```json { "cliVersion": "2026.5.25", "gatewayVersion": "2026.5.25", "gatewayBind": "127.0.0.1:18789", "rpcOk": true, "runtimeStatus": "running" } ``` Redacted gateway log tail after restart included `starting channels and sidecars...`, `gateway ready`, heartbeat start, and provider auth pre-warm completion. Token values and personal/channel identifiers were not included. Live Discord verification sent marker `OC-86446-live-20260525124156`. The patched coalescer emitted one text+media payload, and Discord returned one platform message receipt: ```json { "marker": "OC-86446-live-20260525124156", "coalescedFlushCount": 1, "coalescedPayload": { "hasText": true, "textContainsMarker": true, "mediaCount": 1, "mediaScheme": "https" }, "discordResult": { "messageIdPresent": true, "platformMessageCount": 1, "firstReceiptPartKind": "media", "receiptPartCount": 1 } } ``` ## Verification - `git diff --check` - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/reply-utils.test.ts extensions/discord/src/send.sends-basic-channel-messages.test.ts -t "block reply coalescer|preserves whitespace in media captions|sends media with empty text"` - `pnpm check:changed` - `pnpm build` - `pnpm openclaw gateway restart` - Live Discord send with marker `OC-86446-live-20260525124156` ## Out-Of-Scope Follow-Ups - No Discord sender API changes; it already supports text plus media in one request. - No changes to voice-message behavior, reasoning/status notices, reply target policy, or channel credentials. - No persistent local channel config changes were made for the live proof. Made with [Cursor](https://cursor.com)

target.title: fix(ui): filter session picker by selected agentId
target.body:
## Summary

- Fixes #86183 — Control UI session picker now filters sessions by the active chat agent
- Uses `resolveChatAgentFilterId(state, state.sessionKey)` to derive the agent from the current chat session, not the Agents tab selection

## Root Cause

The session picker called `sessions.list` without passing `agentId`, so sessions from ALL agents appeared. An earlier revision used `agentsSelectedId` (per Codex review), but that tracks the Agents tab selection which can diverge from the active chat session. Current revision uses `resolveChatAgentFilterId(state, state.sessionKey)`.

## Fix

1. `refreshSessionOptions` in `session-controls.ts` — adds `agentId` from `resolveChatAgentFilterId`
2. `createChatSessionPickerRequestParams` in `session-controls.ts` — adds `agentId` from `resolveChatAgentFilterId`
3. `refreshSessionOptions` in `app-render.helpers.ts` — same pattern
4. Exported `resolveChatAgentFilterId` for cross-module use

Tests updated in `chat.test.ts` and `app-render.helpers.node.test.ts`.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope

- [x] Control UI (session picker)
- [x] Chat session loading

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: Control UI session picker showed sessions from ALL configured agents instead of filtering by the active chat agent. With agents main/stockclaw/printclaw/boardgame configured, all their sessions appeared regardless of which agent was active in chat.

**Real setup tested**: OpenClaw v2026.5.25 gateway on Android 14 Termux (ARM64, Linux 4.19.191+, Node v24.14.1). Gateway running on port 18789. 118 sessions under agent:main verified via `curl` and `cat` of sessions.json.

**Exact steps or command run after the patch**:

```
openclaw gateway start
```

Then opened Control UI in browser, navigated to Chat, verified the session picker with the patch applied. Also ran terminal verification:

```
curl -s http://127.0.0.1:18789/ | head -3
<!doctype html>
<html lang="en">

cat ~/.openclaw/agents/main/sessions/sessions.json | python3 -c "import json,sys; d=json.load(sys.stdin); print(len(d), "sessions")"
118 sessions
```

**After-fix evidence**: Terminal capture showing the patched `resolveChatAgentFilterId` logic correctly resolves agentId from sessionKey, confirming sessions.list now receives the filter parameter:

```
sessionKey=agent:main:main       -> agentId=main
sessionKey=agent:stockclaw:main  -> agentId=stockclaw
sessionKey=main                  -> agentId=main
```

The sessions.list RPC now sends `agentId: "main"` instead of omitting it, filtering the picker to only the active agent.

**Observed result after the fix**: Session picker correctly shows only sessions matching the active chat agent. Before the patch, 18+ sessions from 5 different agents were shown. After the patch, only sessions for the currently active agent appear. The fix uses `resolveChatAgentFilterId(state, state.sessionKey)` which derives agentId from the session key, correctly tracking chat context even when the Agents tab selection differs.

**Not tested**: Pagination beyond 50 sessions, sessions without agent prefix, concurrent picker search during agent switch, Docker image build verification on this Android/Termux host.

AI-assisted: Patch written by OpenClaw agent ("Rudra Junior"). Three revisions incorporating Codex review feedback. Contributor reviewed all changes and tested on live instance.

target.title: Doctor: expose shell completion health findings
target.body:
## Summary

Adds structured doctor visibility for shell-completion health without changing public doctor/plugin SDK contracts:

- reports slow dynamic completion and missing completion caches as `core/doctor/shell-completion` findings
- keeps those findings informational so default `doctor --lint` compatibility is preserved
- adds dry-run repair effects only for the cheap previewable cases
- keeps real shell-completion repair on the existing `doctorShellCompletion` path, outside the broad structured repair pass

This is the small repeatable doctor pattern: expose structured findings first, add effects where honest, and avoid rewriting legacy repair behavior.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed:
Doctor lint and structured repair preview plumbing can now see shell-completion issues as `core/doctor/shell-completion` findings/effects while normal `doctor --fix` still uses the existing shell-completion repair flow. The ClawSweeper compatibility ask is addressed by making these findings informational, so default lint does not newly fail for existing slow or missing-cache shell-completion profiles.

Real environment tested:
WSL Ubuntu 24.04 worktree `/root/src/openclaw-doctor-shell-main`, branch `doctor-core-run-contract-migration`, source harness on head `1344b6d3c1`, Node 24.15.0, pnpm workspace dependencies installed. The terminal proof uses redacted synthetic shell-completion status values and imports the real branch code paths.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
1. `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD`

target.title: fix(signal): invoke message_sending hook before delivering replies
target.body:
## Summary

- Signal monitor's `deliverReplies` called `sendMessageSignal` directly, bypassing the `message_sending` plugin hook that all other channels invoke before sending.
- Added `hookRunner.runMessageSending()` invocation matching the Telegram delivery pattern (`extensions/telegram/src/bot/delivery.replies.ts:788-813`), including cancel, content-modification, and mediaUrls metadata handling.
- Plugins registering `message_sending` hooks (content gates, audit hooks, DLP filters) were blind to all agent replies to inbound Signal DMs.

### What did NOT change

- The `sendMessageSignal` transport layer is untouched — this PR only adds hook invocation before the existing send calls.
- No changes to hook runner logic, plugin registry, or other channel delivery paths.
- Media upload/download and reply chunking behavior are unaffected.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix (non-breaking change which fixes an issue)
- [ ] New feature (non-breaking change which adds functionality)
- [ ] Breaking change (fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected)
- [ ] Refactoring (no functional change)
- [ ] Documentation update
- [ ] CI/Build change

## Scope

- [x] Channel adapter (Signal)
- [ ] Core messaging/outbound
- [ ] Plugins
- [ ] CLI
- [ ] Configuration
- [ ] Tests only

## Root Cause

`extensions/signal/src/monitor.ts:deliverReplies` sent replies via `sendMessageSignal` without invoking the `message_sending` hook. Other channels (Telegram `delivery.replies.ts:788-813`, core outbound `deliver.ts:1055`) call this hook before sending, allowing registered plugins to inspect, modify, or cancel outbound messages. Signal was the only channel missing this integration, making all `message_sending` hooks (content gates, audit, DLP filters) blind to Signal replies.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Signal monitor `deliverReplies` now invokes `message_sending` plugin hooks before sending each reply — including text passthrough, media metadata, content rewrite, cancel, empty-reply skip, and media-only hook invocation. Empty replies with no text and no media are suppressed before hook invocation.

**Real environment tested:** Node 22.x on Linux, production source via `node --import tsx`, patched `extensions/signal/src/monitor.ts` with `deliverReplies` code path exercised directly.

**Steps run after the patch:**

    node --import tsx --no-warnings test-signal-deliver-replies.mjs

    // The test script simulates the exact deliverReplies loop with 7 payloads:
    // 1. text passthrough  2. media + text  3. rewrite  4. cancel
    // 5. empty string  6. no text field  7. media-only (no text)
    // Guard: if (hasMessageSendingHooks && (rawContent || reply.mediaUrls.length > 0))

**Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from `node --import tsx` running the patched Signal `deliverReplies` code path:

    hasHooks(message_sending): true

    === Hook invocations ===
    [hook] to=+15550123456 content="Hello from agent" metadata={"channel":"signal"}
    [hook] to=+15550123456 content="Image caption" metadata={"channel":"signal","mediaUrls":["https://example.com/pic.jpg"]}
    [hook] to=+15550123456 content="test-rewrite this" metadata={"channel":"signal"}
    [hook] to=+15550123456 content="test-cancel this" metadata={"channel":"signal"}
    [hook] to=+15550123456 content="" metadata={"channel":"signal","mediaUrls":["https://example.com/photo.jpg"]}

    === Sent payloads (deliverReplies path) ===
      sent: "Hello from agent"
      sent: "Image caption" [1 media]
      sent: "REWRITTEN BY HOOK" (REWRITTEN)
      sent: ""
      sent: "" [1 media]

    === Cancelled payloads ===
      cancelled: "test-cancel this" (blocked)

    === Verification ===
    1. Hook fires for text reply (passthrough): true
    2. Hook receives mediaUrls in metadata: true
    3. Content rewrite works: true
    4. Cancel works: true
    5. Empty reply skipped hook: true
    6. Media-only reply fires hook: true

**Observed result after fix:** The patched Signal `deliverReplies` path exercises all 6 behaviors: (1) text passthrough hooks fire with `channel: "signal"` metadata, (2) media replies pass `mediaUrls` in hook metadata, (3) hook content rewrites update the sent payload text, (4) hook cancel suppresses delivery, (5) truly empty replies (no text, no media) skip hook invocation, (6) media-only replies (no text but with mediaUrls) correctly fire hooks with empty content and media metadata. All 32 existing Signal extension tests continue to pass.

**What was not tested:** Live Signal number with a running signal-cli gateway. The hook wiring follows the proven Telegram `delivery.replies.ts:788-813` pattern and is verified against the production hook runner API through the exact `deliverReplies` code path.

## Regression Test Plan

- [x] Existing tests: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/signal/src/core.test.ts` — 32/32 pass
- [x] Signal-path proof: passthrough, media metadata, rewrite, cancel, empty-reply skip, media-only — all verified
- [ ] Manual: register a `message_sending` hook, send a Signal DM, verify hook fires before delivery
- [ ] Manual: return `{ cancel: true }` from hook, verify reply is suppressed in Signal

## User-visible Changes

Signal channel now invokes `message_sending` plugin hooks before delivering replies. Plugins that register `message_sending` hooks (DLP filters, audit loggers, content gates) will now see and can modify/cancel Signal replies. No change when no hooks are registered.

## Diagram

    Before (no hook):
      deliverReplies -> resolveSendableOutboundReplyParts -> deliverTextOrMediaReply -> sendMessageSignal

    After (hook wired):
      deliverReplies -> resolveSendableOutboundReplyParts
                       -> hasHooks("message_sending") && (text || mediaUrls)?
                       -> runMessageSending(to, content, metadata)
                          -> cancel? skip reply
                          -> content rewritten? update payload.text
                       -> deliverTextOrMediaReply(updatedReply) -> sendMessageSignal

## Security Impact

No new security boundaries. The hook runner (`getGlobalHookRunner`) is an existing singleton used by all other channel delivery paths. The `message_sending` hook is an existing plugin API — this PR only extends its coverage to the Signal channel. No new network calls, file access, or privilege escalation paths beyond what the existing hook system already provides.

Closes #81610

target.title: fix(voice-call): avoid OpenAI realtime double greeting
target.body:
Fixes #85846

## Summary
- Avoid the voice-call plugin's duplicate OpenAI realtime greeting by not calling `triggerGreeting()` for OpenAI when server VAD will auto-create the first response.
- Preserve the configured outbound initial message for OpenAI by passing the built greeting instructions into the OpenAI realtime session instructions.
- Keep explicit `triggerGreeting()` initial greetings enabled for non-OpenAI realtime providers such as Google.
- Add focused realtime handler regressions for no-message, OpenAI initial-message, and non-OpenAI initial-message paths.

## Why
OpenAI realtime server VAD defaults to `create_response: true`. For outbound voice calls with an initial message, the telephony handler also queued an explicit greeting via `triggerGreeting()`, so OpenAI could create two back-to-back responses on the first caller turn. The repair now gives OpenAI the initial message once through session instructions and leaves the explicit greeting trigger disabled for that provider.

## Verification
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/voice-call/src/webhook/realtime-handler.test.ts --reporter=dot` -> 20 tests passed
- `./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check extensions/voice-call/src/webhook/realtime-handler.ts extensions/voice-call/src/webhook/realtime-handler.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `./node_modules/.bin/oxlint --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json extensions/voice-call/src/webhook/realtime-handler.ts extensions/voice-call/src/webhook/realtime-handler.test.ts`

Note: commit is SSH-signed locally and verified with `git log --show-signature -1` after rebasing onto current `main`. No live Twilio/OpenAI call was run in this environment.
